title: Georgina Ford -Hiring Manager Screen - Panzer | Managing Editor
meeting_title: Georgina Ford -Hiring Manager Screen - Panzer | Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hello.
I think you're muted.
It was my audio.
The most common phrase.
I'm good.
Exactly.
Leah, let's average out everything we say to each other on these Zoom calls, and it's, I think you're muted is probably right.
Yeah, I just got off one with a client.
We had a couple of those.
Yeah, it does happen.
Hello, hello.
Pleased to meet you.
Thank you so much for taking the time.
My name is Matthew Panzarino.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
I generally kind of pay attention to content quality and deliverables and work backwards into the systems level thinking that goes into Atlas about how the automated systems are delivering content.
So, that's my idea.
And pleased to meet you.
Nice to have you here.
Happy to chat a little bit about the role.
I've reviewed, of course, your resume and all that stuff, so we don't need to do the history lesson and all of that.
We can async that.
But happy to talk through some things with you.
I'd love to hear, to kind of start off with, a little bit about the way that you judge content quality.
And I think everybody's got their own kind of internal rubrics or methods or methodologies that they've come up with over time.
I shouldn't say everybody.
Everybody in content, like yourself, and has worked with clients and multiple stakeholders probably has a rationale for this.
So we'll just hear about that editorial judgment and quality standards piece.
How do you personally judge whether a piece of content is high quality?
How do you know when it's good enough?
And how do you calibrate the needs to a client?
So I'd love to hear anything about that aspect.
Sure.
Obviously, first step, my first step when reviewing a piece of content is to read through it.
And that's my first indication, whether it's a piece of good content or not, is whether I can concentrate past line one and two, if it hooks me, really.
And I think that is, and I believe you have a journalistic background as well.
I think it's one of the things that we often miss in some marketing content is that immediate hook that gets people to want to read the rest of it.
So in my view, it's can I read it is point one.
Point two is, is it telling me anything important?
Or is it just because we do get a lot of kind of marketing content that's a thinly veiled product pitch that doesn't have a hook or a meaning to anybody.
It's, and I know they, we are, there's blogs and stuff created for SEO and those kind of purposes.
But if we're creating content that actually needs to mean something.
needs to have a meaning or a pain point or a point to it.
So I kind of have a four-step process.

So first I read through it and check if it hooks me, if it's interesting, if I notice any grammar errors, like a missing Oxford comma or Oxford commas in a client editorial guideline that does not have Oxford commas.
All the basics, really.
But my first thing is to read through and note those things.
Then things like listicle sentences as well, which AI is very good at doing, but it's like it's always three things followed by three things and they don't necessarily have a meaning or go deeper.
So it's just a list.
So I look for those kind of things that are flags to me.
So that's, I read through it first.
I look for all those things.
I note those things.
And then I start by doing the grammar and spelling.
Then I go.
And I use Grammarly generally for grammar and spelling because it's better than me.
If I have to review 50 pieces of content, I might miss a comma, right?
And we're all human.
And then I read through for context, meaning.
Then I read through for client guidelines.
So a good piece of content to me.
And it doesn't have to come from the writer 100% perfect straight away.
And if it does, that's amazing.
But it needs to flow.
It needs to meet client guidelines.
And it needs to hook me in a way that's appropriate for the client and what their goals are.
As I said, there's a lot of marketing content that's...
It doesn't hook you.
It's a statement, you know?
So that's kind of an overview of my process.
And then when you're, I mean, obviously, working with clients and on deadlines, especially deliverables...
At scale, there's always a tension between, okay, this thing could be better because there's no piece of content that's ever been created that's perfect.
Maybe, I don't know, we could argue about some novels or poems, but by and large, most marketing content is not perfect.
It could always be polished further.
And so talk to me about how you kind of like feel that tension between, okay, we need to deliver this, and it serves the needs.
But, of course, it could be better.
You know, the instinct would always be everything could be better.

So talk to me about the tension there, how you feel that enough, and when that hardens for you to a place where you're like, hey, this is great, could be better, but it's good enough, and we're going to ship, right?
Yeah.
Again, it really depends on client goals.
Let's say there's timelines, right?
I'm not going to send out a piece that is, that, as I said, doesn't hook me, has mistakes, but there's something, as you say, that...
It's good enough to go.
And I don't know what your process is in terms of review cycles with the client, because obviously there's that ability to help shape it a little more during that process.
If we're talking about once that process is closed, it sometimes just comes down to whether the client is happy with it.
Yeah.
And sometimes that's the best that you can do in that moment.
Is the client happy with it?
Yes, it could be better.
But you know what?
Let's ship it.
I've had pieces where there's been 28 client reviews, reviewers in a piece, so it's never going to be good enough.
But it gets to a point where you're like, okay, we're going to ship it like it is.
Yeah.
Thank you, by the way, for giving me some new nightmares to think about.
You're welcome.
It's the most amount of reviewers I think I've ever seen in a single document.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, mean, I can't, you know, I've seen it balloon.
But 28 is, whew, that's enormous.
Yeah, we have a client that has an MD, an ND, a lawyer, and a co-founder, right?
I mean, we have ones with more as well, but a large amount of those are more marketing people that are, you know, looking for SEO and, you know, basic goals.
But the MD and ND ones are tough, you know, because they don't let anything go, nor should they, really, you know, we should be able to deliver.

But, so talk to me a little bit about, like, editorial processes, because we, we're a very process-driven org, given that we do operate at scale, we kind of approach content from an abundance mindset.

So we often are able to tell a client, hey, we can take more shots on goal than you ever thought possible with content, because it's expensive and it's hard to produce, et cetera, typically, you know, traditionally.
But that means that internally, our processes have to be pretty strong, because we, you know, kind of high-level deliverables.
High level of quality expectations, and then we work with a combination of AI tooling and human intervention.

So I'd love to hear a little bit about you on your, like, a content or editorial process that you saw room for improvement, proposed or affected improvements, and then, you know, kind of what was the good bits that resulted, and what was the hardest part of that?
So just reach back to your practical experience, and what was an opportunity to do that that you saw and took?
So I can directly apply that to my current job.
When I got there, there was no formal process at all, and that was leading to client delays, deliverables not being delivered.
Our content and creative team didn't have a schedule.
So, you know, somebody would say, hey, can you design this for me on a Monday?
When's it due?
Oh, tomorrow.
Yeah.
And design people.
hours, yeah.
And writers and design teams can get a little frustrated when they have, you know, 10 different project managers.
Just saying, hey, here's the thing, need it tomorrow, with no fire discussion or anything else.
So I built out, well, I've done it on two platforms now.
I built out an Asana workflow that combined, sorry, resourcing, project management, and communications with our creative team.
So I built out, we have a kickoff, like 10 tasks you have to do at kickoff.
So get the background materials from the client.
For our AI stuff, it's make sure you get at least two background pieces of background material from the client, for example.
Set up your kickoff call with the client so that you can talk through things.
Set up your Google Drive folders.
Set up your resource request forms for your writer and your designer, et cetera.
I'm not going to list all of them.
And then you have to tick them off.
And then we go into the copy creation.
So we have one of
The first task is creating your SME questions, then having the SME call, and then we have three rounds of reviews, and obviously design and writing are integrated into that process.
Once I had implemented that, we had a measured 25% reduction in team time to deliver, because we now had specific dates outlined.
I created templates for all of our different content types based in Asana.
We had specific workflows.
We knew when things were supposed to be being delivered, and we had reminders as well.
So it's like, hey, don't forget, tomorrow you've got to do a thing.
And there was a little friction rolling it out the first time, because people are like, well, this is, I'm not, why do I need to do this?
And it's like, well, because the creative team needs you to do this, because they need to know what's coming, and we need to be efficient for the clients.
So 25% reduction in team time to delivery, a huge, huge lift.
In just being more effective with clients and clients being happy with us.
sorry.
I just completely forgot the word.
Client satisfaction.
Yeah, client satisfaction.
There we go.
I always remember Tim Cook, customer sat.
He's always obsessed about customer sat.
Yeah.
Yes, yes.
Got it.
And obviously, we include review cycles in their internal and client review cycles.
We then moved it over to another internal program.
But we were able to, something that was very helpful to the way that we worked, was the ability to create client view timelines.
So the client can check in on the project at any point in time.
And obviously, the dates move dynamically.
So if they delayed by two days, they could see the effect on the entire timeline.
So having that as a tool to like, hey, here's where we're at.
I'm sorry you delayed by two days, but now your delivery date's potentially going to be pushed back.
If you want it here, then we're going to have to, you're going to have to be more efficient on
So it was more of a, it was a visual aid to help them understand how we worked and how we needed them to work to best have the result that they needed in the timeframe that they needed.
I'm a little bit of a nerd about process.
That's good.
That's great.
I love process nerds.
I am one too.
Guilty.
That's great.
And then, you know, I think it's a natural segue to, you know, in that process or in previous processes, of course, and you've mentioned Grammarly already.
But talk to me about like the way you use AI tooling or, you know, the way that your org is using it.
And obviously you are setting the standards for and the process for, so it can be you personally using it or, of course, the system using it.
You know, what was the, what were the problems that you had to solve with them, whether it's content scale or quality or anything like that?
And then, yeah, just talk me through a little bit of that.
Just love to hear what kind of AI tooling you've used in what capacities and what were the problems you're trying to
Sure.
So I created our AI content program for my current role, primarily based in Gemini and ChatGPT.
The problems we were trying to solve was the clients wanted things faster and cheaper.
And I think that's something across most clients is content is great, but we want it fast.
We want it cheap.
And we want to be more efficient with our time to market.
So I have created custom gems and GPTs to ensure that everybody across the organization, no matter their comfort level with AI, can utilize these gems to produce strong output.
Obviously, we do have the human review cycles as well.
So, for example, we kick off with a client, we gather all their background information.
I use that information and their website to do a company analysis and competitor differentiation research gem report, which gives me quite accurate information about their positioning in the market.
I have comparison tables, so they have direct comparisons to their competitors, so you know what areas to highlight in their content.

And everything is referenced as well, so all the links are referenced and linked, and obviously, I prefer nothing older than 18 months, so I make sure that it's told to use new content rather than something from 2020.
So I have that as a starting point.
I also have an industry analysis gem, so we input the company website, the vertical, and the target audience, and it gives you an overview of what the trends are in the industry.
Pain points for target audiences остр
I also have a persona creator.
So, again, using the website background information, it creates between three and five.
You had to tell it how many you want.
Very specific personas.
So it'll be like a CTO named Greg Wilson who lives in the Wine Valleys of California.
He's 49 years old.
He went to Harvard.
So very in-depth personas.
And we use that information to then help build the content.
That was a lot of words.
I'm sorry.
I'm also a little bit of an AI nerd.
I completely follow him because, you know, it's obviously we have our own tooling, but very similar process.
And then we do use, I think that plenty of people use whatever they want.
And we're relatively agnostic, you know, wherever gets the job done.
But for team coherence, we generally recommend people use like Cloud Projects as an example because we've built out, you know, some projects for people.
But honestly, the application of it is very.
Very similar to a custom GPT or a GEM.
They're all relatively similar.
You have project context and then memory, and then you have established processes that they can reference.
They can see what has been done and what is being done and the steps to get a relatively consistent result.

Our actual content is created in our own tooling, but the post-processing or, frankly, prototyping new processes that you think, hey, maybe this needs to exist in our product, can often happen in tools like that.
So it's good that you, you know, this is a very similar process to what we use.
The only difference being the brand name at the top.
Yeah.
And then, obviously, we have the review cycles, which is both the creative team or the writing team and your, we're the CS team.
Yeah.
We do the review cycles as well because we've got different perspectives.
And then we do, we have shortened review cycles.
We have shortened D1, D2.
Cycles, et cetera.
We've had very happy clients so far.
And again, we've had about a 20% reduction in costs.
Nice.
What was the kind of big pivotal thing for you, or just pick one, that you learned during the process of setting that up, getting it architected, and then seeing people begin to use it?
Like, what was the big revelation for you, either in terms of like, oh, wow, this is a big unlock, or ooh, this is actually a thornier problem than I thought.
So I'm really agnostic about the negative versus positive, just like something that you realized during the process.
I think initially, I was not prepared for the huge variation in competence or understanding of AI.
So it was one of the challenges that I had to work out and build in was usability.
So they, because we have people from all walks.

of life, that usability, it needed to be, so, for example, my, the things that I've built, you just type in hi, and then it tells you exactly what it needs, and then you put it in, and then it gives you the output, because some people can write code, and some people are just starting to use ChatGPT.
Right, they just had ChatGPT for something for the first time last week, right?

Yeah, so that was a learning curve for me, in trying to make sure that everybody, it was accessible to everybody, but also, you know, one of the funny things is, when you're dealing with clients, they may not always be content experts, right?
So, they don't always, I mean, they know that you're using AI, but I don't think a lot of them...
substitute almost never, by the way, it's just us, here, so...
Yeah, so...
It's rare, it's nice, it's...
nice when it happens, but it's great.
So to be honest, I mean, are they really seeing a difference in content quality?
And there's that as well.
Like, obviously, we want it to be the very best it can be.
But also, I don't think they notice a huge, they don't know the difference or notice the difference between AI created and human created.
So there's an interesting tension there.
As to how to market it or how to externalize the fact you're using AI in a way that it makes it sound equally as valuable as human written.
And I think that's a challenge because, you know, you see everything online and in social media and AI is terrible at writing content.
So that's what people have internalized, whether they know content or not.
And it's being able to change that.
Perception to, oh, actually, this meets all our goals, and it's actually a really nice piece of content.
And, oh, it sounds like me, too.
That's cool.
You know?
We have, I agree, it's a tension, and it's an interesting problem to unknot, especially when people have made an attempt internally.

Like, if a client has made an attempt internally to generate content with using, you know, one of the dozens of SaaS tools or some combination of automations internally to create content and essentially failed at it, you know, or failed to generate quality content, they come in with a bias because they're like, hey, everything I've done with this is bad.
Therefore, this is probably going to be bad, you know, whatever you produce for me.
And it's not, but, you know, they come in with their own biases, and you have to sometimes punch through them.
So, we've seen it happen.
Usually, what you do is you produce, and you produce outcomes, and you say, hey, we're driving to outcomes.
Then they're cool.
But that takes three months.
It can, depending on how it hits.
Great.
I think that's most of my questions.
I'm happy to open it up to you, if you have any questions for me.
Happy to answer any questions about the way we work or what we're up to.
I have questions.
I even printed them out.
So how do you measure the effectiveness of the content that you produce?
Do you follow up with metrics?
How do you measure the content?
Yes.
All of the standard ways that you would measure content, so SEO, LLM visibility, traffic, clicks, etc., paired with our overlying umbrella metric, which is does it drive to the customer's outcome?
So we're relatively agnostic about how we get to the outcomes, and we have to sometimes educate clients, just like we have to educate them about the shots on goal changes and the abundance mindset.

Change that you have to undergo when you're working with LLM content, because I can produce in the same, in a month's time, I can produce the work of dozens of writers that would cost them millions of dollars to like actually produce well-researched, fact-checked, solid content that generates traffic and clicks.

So when you're thinking of it in those terms, it can be difficult sometimes to like map, you know, clicks and impressions or conversions, demo conversions, all of that stuff to the outcomes until they start to see the outcomes.
And then you're like, okay, cool.

So really generally what we try to do is establish a really straightforward outcome that they want that is growth related instead of, hey, we will get you 10,000 page views or we will get you a million clicks or whatever it is.
Who cares?
What is your actual outcome?
And because we can take more shots on goal and create more permutations and angles and facets to their content strategy than would be possible.
Otherwise, we are able.
We to shape our content strategy into more of a goals-oriented approach, our outcomes-oriented approach, instead of week-by-week or month-by-one traffic goals.
That said, yes, they do have a dashboard, they can see those things, they can see the progress towards those things.

We do concentrate on very similar top lines as any agency would, like a LLM visibility increase, increase in overall traffic, or if they provide us the ability to, like either a Google tag or even a deeper integration into their conversion funnel, are we driving conversions for you, qualified leads, et cetera.
So the needs vary per client, as you can imagine, what their desired outcomes are, but the outcome that all of them really desire is growth or, you know, that whatever way they look at growth.
And so we try to keep the conversation higher level versus, oh, we will, you know, promise, we don't write contracts based on like number of clicks or traffic-driven in general.
We get established.
Goals with the client, but in all reality, our contract SOPs are around like, hey, we're going to produce roughly this much output for you each month driving towards this outcome.
And of course, we keep ourselves on it by like week by week evaluating internally, producing monthly reports for the client, and then they have access to a dashboard of continuous information.
Okay.
I'm very interested to see that and how it works.
I find the AI part really interesting.
Sorry.
Sidebar.
What level of experimentation and innovation is encouraged for editorial direction?
So if I come and I say, so I've had an idea.
What about this?
Infinite.
Oh, yeah.
We don't, we don't want to, like, at the moment, there is a blend of, a blend of.
There are skill sets that are needed, and the room for flexibility within the framework of achieving an outcome for a client is extremely high right now.
So it's enormously elastic.
If you lean into the process, you will find opportunities to engage in the process, improve the process.
Nothing is set in stone.
The documentation is incredibly transparent across the organization.
Nine-tenths of the Slack channels are open, so you can see the way people are working with clients and things that they're doing.
We do try and share that information across the company so that people are like, oh, you know, I found this new lever to pull.
And they're like, oh my gosh, I didn't even thought of pulling that lever, right?
And so we try to bring that to the editorial strategy component as well.
We don't want people to say, hey, I'm going to deliver these pieces, but I have a really strong feeling this is not going to drive towards goal.
Oh, okay, yeah, that's fine.
Be quiet.
You know, no.
Because at the end of the day, we're on.
For results.
And we don't want to, we're still relatively young, but we are getting up on yearly renewals for some, you know, like large clients.
And we look at the results that we've driven for them.
We're pretty comfortable with that.
Those decisions that led to this point were made months ago.
So if we had waited until now to like make large changes or whatever, then we would have a major problem.
But we often, often come up with ideas internally in groups or pods at the Emmy layer or even below that to just say like, hey, we've got some ideas for new shops.
And generally speaking, clients are, they love it, right?
They're happy to have more work, more information because they can't afford to do it either time-wise or cost-wise or whatever internally.
And for us, it's a matter of like, hey, I see this new pipeline that we just built for this other client.
I think we can repurpose that.
Or modify that or adapt that and then produce similar work or a new kind of work for this client.
And maybe that's just an experiment or a case study that we do with them and they're happy to have it because, it's more shots on goal.
Or maybe it turns into something that's a new work stream and we can upsell them and say, hey, we've got a brand new work stream for you.
We have created three directories for clients and they're producing real demonstrable results.
I see this opportunity for a content bucket in your world that needs a directory.
did some research.
Nobody's really built this before.
If we build it for you, it'll be first.
There'll be a link magnet.
We'll do all this.
That's like a couple million dollar project that we can probably spin up in a matter of weeks and start delivering on because we use AI workflows.
Okay.
I was going to ask your AO and SEO, do you use additional tools for that or is that also built into your AI?
Bye.
Um, yeah, the requirements or, um, best practices for generating those results are built into the pipelines.
As far as measuring and the reciprocal flow, we have built our own tool.
Um, it's not yet, it is soft launched.
So theoretically you could find it, but I'm not going to tell you.
No, I'm kidding.
Um, we're working on it.
It's, we're going to hard launch it very soon, but we built our own tool like scrunch or, or, what are the other ones?
There's innumerate other ones.
I just saw Amplish who launched one, but anyhow, everybody's got a monitoring tool.
Um, but most of them are doing it from the metric side or in complete isolation from the content.
And we, we have a real chance to offer something with grounded results that is driven from like what we have actually seen.
across dozens of clients and from a granular content level.
Like we can tell you whether.
If this sentence would work better for Geo or not, eventually, I can't, I'm not promising we can do that at this moment, but we have a real opportunity to do that.
So we are launching our own tooling to do it.
That sounds very exciting.
Where do you see the biggest gap in how marketers are applying AI in their content workflows and strategy?
And is there a gap that you have that you feel could be filled better?
I think I alluded to it a little bit earlier, but I honestly think it's granularity.
I think a lot of times because people have a lean or starvation mindset when it comes to content purely because of the cost and time.
They end up thinking that way and their frameworks for making decisions about what opportunities to pursue are based on that.
So they miss the forest for the trees, the literal forest where you're like, hey, we can plant blades of grass for you in this forest, right?
Versus, oh, we've got to establish, you know, a perimeter and try and hope anybody in that forest sort of likes our fence.
You know, it's a really different way of thinking about attacking an audience.
And you can do things that just simply wouldn't scale or that you had to make a really big choice sort of at the beginning of a project.
And I'm sure you've been in on those discussions where they're like going in like, okay, we know we need content, right?
We don't have an organic content operation at all.
And, you we've come to you and we're hoping to get something spun up or whatever.
And at the beginning of those conversations, it is largely about, okay, look, here's your overall budget that you've given us or that we've given you.
And within that budget, we can either.
Produce like top of funnel or a slice of top to mid funnel, or we can produce mid funnel, or we can produce, you know, this low funnel.
And depending on how much budget you have within those slices, you can then afford to take a half a dozen bets a quarter, right?
Approximately, or maybe with AI in the past, you could increase that maybe a couple of dozen bets a quarter.
Like we're publishing 30 pieces a week for some clients.
And like that, like, and that, are mostly programmatic in nature.

Editorial, I would say, generally speaking, the peak is at the moment is about 10 to 15, you know, editorial pieces, but that's still an enormous, that's like a factorial of what most people normally produce.
This allows you the opportunity to say, hey, do you want to talk to like nurses in Alabama that work the swing shift?
Sure, no problem.
Like, I'll have an article for you about that next week, and we'll see what happens over the next few weeks.
Like...
Like...
Like...
Cool.
Oh, not the swing shift.
You want the morning shift.
No problem.
Yeah, we'll have one of those too, right?
Like, I think it's, and I'm exaggerating a little bit because granularity is somewhat governed by the web, you know, and its ability to determine those audiences.

But it's, it's remarkable how your mindset changes when you realize like, oh, I can leave a narrative here that's more complex and nuanced across all of the content on my site to like send signals out to, of course, search engines, and of course, LLMs, but also to people that we are authorities in this space and that we can speak to them directly and that we understand their needs and desires well enough to write an article that has super granular in-network information that sends the signal to the human, like, these people must know my life.
Like, this is how I live.
These are the things I encounter every day.
And of course, the most talented editors and writers can do this on any one piece.
I'm not claiming AI could do any of those better.
If I hired the best writers and the best editors...
And paired them with SMEs or domain experts and plenty of time to research those things, they could absolutely turn out articles that are just as good or better than what we produce.
It's not some miracle content.
It's just they can't do it 30 times a week.
They can't do it 10 times a week.
It's just impossible.
So that's the way we look at opportunities, the gap in opportunities.
It's not so much like we found this magical new hole that we can fill until Google builds a product that ignores it or takes it into account, which is what all SEO is.
know, it's like, I found out a chick in the armor.
You know, I found this gap that I could drive my spear into for like the next six weeks until Google patches it.
It's not, you know, it's building like that domain authority word by word and sentence by sentence.
I don't know if that's more granular than what previously available.
I think that's what's so exciting about it is there is so much experimentation and so many iterations and different things that you can play with.
And try and be successful with as well.
Because it's kind of a new frontier, really.
Yeah.
It is a new frontier, not kind of.
Yeah, no, it is.
It's pretty exciting.
And then, like, I have to jump to another one, and I'm sure you've got to go too.
But, like, the cool, fun bit that I think keeps you, it's like, okay, getting the client to work out and stuff has its own reward.
That's great.
Business keeps growing.
We're all good.
And they get results, which is great.

But the internal-facing aspect of this role and, like, the sort of thing that would invigorate somebody to come work here is that we are doing things with LLMs and with that architecture that are at the edge of what is actually possible.
And we absolutely not going to sugarcoat it.
At times, our front wheels are dangling off the edge, right?
And we just hope that a rear-wheel drive works.
Not to stretch the metaphor too far.
But, like, it is, it's, we're at the edge.
And when it works,
It's like, you know, it's beautiful.
And then sometimes it doesn't work.
And you have to step in and go like, okay, how can this process be improved?
How can the artifacts be improved?
How can the loop between the humans and the machinery be recorded, articulated, and understood so that we can then improve the process?
How do I make my job easier tomorrow?
Yeah.
And then you do that day after day after day after day.

The beauty of it and the invigorating part of it is that I worked in editorial for 20 years, and that process either lived in humans or in a moment in time where I gave an instruction to somebody, or maybe at best in a set of Google Docs on a drive.
If you found them, you found the mother load, and then you had to understand them and grok them in the context with which they were delivered and all of that, right?
So we've all seen it.
You have a brilliant editorial mind as a part of your organization and it's working really well.
And they leave, and you're really happy to see them moving on, new career stuff.
But now you're like, now I have a huge job.
It's process-oriented whole that I need to be able to articulate better.
And that's what we're building.
That sounds awesome.
And I know we're over time.
I was wondering about Next Steps.
Also, a fellow editorial.
Middle East, actually.
While I started off in South Africa, I wrote to university, interning with Reuters.
And then I was managing editor for five finance – the first female managing editor in the UAE for five financial publications, brands.
We had events, social, digital.
That could not have been boring.
Print.
No.
Yeah, that could not have been boring at all.
Yeah.
Yeah, Next Steps, Marilia will reach back out.
She will hit you up.
And then I believe you'll end up speaking with one of our directors, Megan, as well.
And you'll be able to get her perspective on things, which may be a little different later, a slice of the org than me.
So that'll get you a little bit more perspective, and then we'll go from there.
Okay, perfect.
And thank you very much for your time, and I'm sorry we went over a little.
That's okay.
Thank you very much.
Appreciate it.
Have a good day.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Screening Georgina Ford for the Managing Editor role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=399.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Process-Driven:** Georgina built an Asana workflow that cut content delivery time by 25% by standardizing tasks, creating templates, and providing client-viewable timelines to manage expectations.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=804.0)
  - [**AI-Native:** Developed a custom AI content program using Gemini/ChatGPT, building user-friendly "Gems" for tasks like company analysis and persona creation to ensure adoption across varying team skill levels.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1103.0)
  - [**GrowthX's AI Model:** GrowthX uses proprietary AI to produce high-volume, granular content (e.g., 30 pieces/week) that drives client growth outcomes, shifting from a "starvation mindset" to an "abundance mindset."](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1642.0)
  - [**Cultural Fit:** Georgina's background as the first female Managing Editor for five financial publications in the UAE and her enthusiasm for process innovation align with GrowthX's culture.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=2531.0)

## Topics

### Georgina's Approach to Content Quality

  - [**Core Philosophy:** Content must be engaging and meaningful, not just a thinly veiled product pitch.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=439.0)
  - [**4-Step Review Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=439.0)
    1.  [**Read-Through:** Assess initial hook and flow. Flag AI-generated patterns (e.g., listicle sentences).](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
    2.  [**Grammar & Spelling:** Use Grammarly for efficiency at scale.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=595.0)
    3.  [**Context & Meaning:** Ensure the message is clear and impactful.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=613.0)
    4.  [**Client Guidelines:** Verify adherence to brand voice and style.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=616.0)
  - [**Balancing Quality & Deadlines:**](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=653.0)
      - [The "good enough" threshold is met when content is error-free, flows well, and satisfies client goals.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=619.0)
      - [Client satisfaction is the ultimate arbiter, especially with complex review cycles (e.g., 28 reviewers on one piece).](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=732.0)

### Process Improvement & AI Implementation

  - [**Problem:** A lack of formal process at Georgina's current role caused client delays and team frustration.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=867.0)
  - [**Solution:** Built an Asana workflow to standardize the content lifecycle, from kickoff to final delivery.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=867.0)
      - [**Key Features:**](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=925.0)
          - [Standardized kickoff tasks.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=925.0)
          - [Content-type-specific templates.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=986.0)
          - [Integrated writing and design workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=956.0)
          - [Client-viewable timelines to manage expectations.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1045.0)
  - [**Result:** A measured 25% reduction in team time to delivery and increased client satisfaction.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=972.0)
  - [**AI Program Development:**](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1103.0)
      - [**Goal:** Deliver content faster and cheaper.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1152.0)
      - [**Method:** Created custom "Gems" (Gemini/ChatGPT) for specific tasks.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1168.0)
          - [**Company Analysis Gem:** Generates reports on market positioning and competitor differentiation.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1193.0)
          - [**Industry Analysis Gem:** Provides overviews of trends and audience pain points.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1245.0)
          - [**Persona Creator Gem:** Builds detailed personas (e.g., "CTO Greg Wilson, 49, Harvard grad").](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)
      - [**Result:** \~20% reduction in costs.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1384.0)
  - [**Key Learning:** AI tools must be highly usable (e.g., simple prompts) to ensure adoption across a team with varying technical skill levels.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1412.0)

### GrowthX's AI-Driven Content Strategy

  - [**Core Philosophy:** An "abundance mindset" enabled by proprietary AI tooling.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1642.0)
      - [**Capability:** Produce high-volume, high-quality content (e.g., 30 pieces/week) at a scale impossible with traditional methods.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=2116.0)
  - [**Strategic Focus:** Granular content for niche audiences (e.g., "nurses on the swing shift in Alabama").](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=2265.0)
      - [**Rationale:** Builds deep domain authority and sends strong signals to search engines and LLMs.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=2294.0)
  - [**Measurement:**](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1642.0)
      - [**Primary:** Client growth outcomes (e.g., qualified leads, conversions).](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1642.0)
      - [**Secondary:** Standard metrics (SEO, traffic, clicks) tracked via a client dashboard.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1642.0)
  - [**Innovation Culture:**](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1835.0)
      - [**Encouraged:** Team members are expected to experiment and propose process improvements.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1835.0)
      - [**Mechanism:** Transparent documentation and open Slack channels facilitate knowledge sharing.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1875.0)
      - [**Opportunity:** Repurpose successful workflows for new client projects or upsells.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=1974.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Marilia:** Contact Georgina to schedule the next interview.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=2531.0)
  - [**Georgina:** Interview with Megan (Director) to get a different organizational perspective.](https://fathom.video/share/ew2gmPtoXEjtazyqoxTDs7BXwBJTaSrs?tab=summary&timestamp=2564.0)

action_items.description: Email Georgina re: next steps; schedule director interview w/ Megan
